threats.title: burn() Reentrancy via Token Transfer Hooks Enables Complete Pool Drain
threats.hypothesis_description: burn() executes both IERC20(userToken).transfer and IERC20(validatorToken).transfer BEFORE updating liquidityBalances, totalSupply, or pool reserves. If either token implements transfer hooks (ERC-777 tokensReceived, ERC-1363, or any callback-on-receive token), the recipient contract can reenter burn() with the same unchanged liquidityBalances, burning the same LP tokens repeatedly to extract multiples of their fair share. Pool creation is permissionless with no token whitelist, so an attacker can deploy a hook-enabled token, create a pool, and exploit once other LPs deposit.
threats.investigation_rationale: The checks-effects-interactions pattern is violated in the most dangerous way possible: TWO external calls before ANY state update. Each reentrant call sees identical pre-burn state, enabling N-fold extraction where N is limited only by gas/stack depth.

proof_of_signal.pattern: IERC20(userToken).transfer(to, amountUserToken) and IERC20(validatorToken).transfer(to, amountValidatorToken) execute BEFORE liquidityBalances[poolId][msg.sender] -= liquidity, totalSupply[poolId] -= liquidity, pool.reserveUserToken -= uint128(amountUserToken), pool.reserveValidatorToken -= uint128(amountValidatorToken)

preconditions: Pool uses at least one token with transfer hooks (ERC-777, ERC-1363, or custom callback)
Attacker holds LP tokens (liquidityBalances > 0) in the target pool
Pool has reserves from other LPs to drain beyond attacker's fair share
Sufficient gas for reentrant call depth

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: amountUserToken = (liquidity * pool.reserveUserToken) / _totalSupply computed once, but extractable N times before state update
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Each reentrant burn() call sees unchanged liquidityBalances[msg.sender], totalSupply, and pool reserves, extracting the same proportional amount each time
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Profit = (N - 1) × (liquidity / totalSupply) × totalPoolValue, where N = number of reentrant calls

economic_questions: What is the maximum reentrant depth before gas exhaustion on the target L2?
Are any TIP-20 stablecoins ERC-777 compatible or have transfer hooks?
What is the total TVL at risk across all pools?
validation_requirements: Deploy proof-of-concept with ERC-777 token and verify multi-burn extraction
Measure gas cost per reentrant level on target chain
Verify Solidity 0.8 checked arithmetic on final unwinding doesn't prevent outer calls from completing

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: Minimal initial LP deposit (e.g., 1000 tokens) plus ERC-777 token deployment (~0.01 ETH)
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_execution_cost: Gas for reentrant calls, estimated 500K-2M gas on L2 (~$0.10-$1.00)
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Up to 100% of pool reserves beyond attacker's own deposit
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: >10000% ROI if pool has significant other LP deposits

market_conditions_analysis.liquidity_requirements: No external liquidity needed; exploit is internal to the pool
market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: After significant LP deposits accumulate in the target pool
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Pool is completely drained; all other LPs lose their entire deposit

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: All other LPs in the pool
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Entire pool TVL minus attacker's own deposit

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: No reentrancy guard, no CEI compliance, state updates at lines 212-215 after transfers at lines 207-208
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Verify that uint128 underflow on reserve subtraction during unwinding doesn't revert outer calls if attacker sizes withdrawals correctly

threats.title: executeFeeSwap() Reentrancy via Malicious userToken Drains All validatorToken Reserves
threats.hypothesis_description: executeFeeSwap() calls IERC20(userToken).transferFrom(msg.sender, address(this), amountIn) BEFORE updating pool.reserveUserToken and pool.reserveValidatorToken. If userToken is a token with a sender hook (ERC-777 tokensToSend), the attacker's hook fires during transferFrom, allowing reentry into executeFeeSwap. Since reserveValidatorToken has not been decremented, the sufficiency check passes on each reentrant call. Each call transfers out amountOut of validatorToken. The attacker deploys their own hook-enabled token as userToken, creates a pool with a real stablecoin as validatorToken, and drains all validatorToken after LPs deposit.
threats.investigation_rationale: The transferFrom external call precedes both state updates. An attacker-controlled userToken can execute arbitrary code during transferFrom, including reentering executeFeeSwap N times, each time extracting validatorTokens against the unchanged (original) reserve balance.

proof_of_signal.pattern: IERC20(userToken).transferFrom at line 114 executes before pool.reserveUserToken += at line 116 and pool.reserveValidatorToken -= at line 117; reserve sufficiency check at line 112 uses stale value on reentry

preconditions: Attacker deploys a hook-enabled token (ERC-777 or custom) as userToken
Pool exists between attacker's token and a valuable validatorToken
Pool has validatorToken reserves deposited by LPs
Attacker's token transferFrom fires callback before completing

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: if (pool.reserveValidatorToken < amountOut) revert InsufficientLiquidity() — this check uses stale reserveValidatorToken on each reentrant call
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Each reentrant executeFeeSwap call passes the same reserve check since reserveValidatorToken -= amountOut hasn't executed yet
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Total extraction = N × amountOut where N = floor(original_reserve / amountOut). Cost = 0 if malicious token transferFrom doesn't actually transfer.

economic_questions: Can the attacker's malicious transferFrom return true without actually transferring tokens?
What's the maximum N before gas/stack limits on the target L2?
Does the final state unwinding (N decrements of reserveValidatorToken) underflow?
validation_requirements: Build malicious ERC-777 token that reenters executeFeeSwap on transferFrom
Test full drain scenario with N reentrant calls
Verify that Solidity 0.8 checked arithmetic doesn't save the protocol on unwinding

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: Initial LP deposit of validatorToken to seed pool + gas for deployment (<$10 on L2)
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_execution_cost: Gas for reentrant calls (~$0.10-$2.00 on L2)
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: 100% of validatorToken reserves minus attacker's own seed deposit

market_conditions_analysis.liquidity_requirements: None external; attacker needs LPs to have deposited into the pool
market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: After pool accumulates significant validatorToken deposits
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Complete drain of validatorToken reserves from the pool

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: All LPs who deposited validatorToken into the pool
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Entire validatorToken reserve in the targeted pool

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: amountOut = amountIn * 9970 / 10000; attacker chooses amountIn to maximize extraction per call
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: On unwinding, reserveValidatorToken is decremented N times: verify N*amountOut <= original reserve to avoid revert

threats.title: rebalanceSwap() Optimistic State Update Enables Phantom Reserve Exploitation via Cross-Function Reentrancy
threats.hypothesis_description: rebalanceSwap() updates pool.reserveValidatorToken += amountIn and pool.reserveUserToken -= amountOut BEFORE calling IERC20(validatorToken).transferFrom. If validatorToken has hooks, the attacker's callback fires after reserves are optimistically inflated but before tokens actually arrive. The attacker reenters burn() during this callback: the inflated reserveValidatorToken causes _calculateBurnAmounts to return more validatorToken than actually exists in the contract. The attacker extracts phantom reserves, making the pool insolvent.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is uniquely dangerous because state is updated BEFORE the external call (optimistic update), meaning the reentered function sees INFLATED reserves, not stale ones. burn() proportionally distributes based on these inflated reserves, transferring more validatorToken than the contract actually holds.

proof_of_signal.pattern: pool.reserveValidatorToken += uint128(amountIn) at line 150 and pool.reserveUserToken -= uint128(amountOut) at line 151 execute BEFORE IERC20(validatorToken).transferFrom at line 153; reserves reflect tokens not yet received

preconditions: validatorToken has transfer hooks (ERC-777 tokensToSend on sender side during transferFrom)
Attacker holds LP tokens in the target pool
Pool has sufficient reserves for the burn to extract meaningful value
Attacker can trigger callback during transferFrom

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: After optimistic update: reserveValidatorToken = original + amountIn (inflated). burn's _calculateBurnAmounts: amountValidatorToken = liquidity * inflatedReserveVT / totalSupply
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Attacker chooses large amountIn in rebalanceSwap to maximally inflate reserveValidatorToken. During callback, burns LP tokens against inflated reserves. Contract transfers out validatorTokens it doesn't actually have.
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Profit ≈ (amountIn / totalSupply) × liquidity_held, extracted from other LPs' deposits

economic_questions: Can the attacker's transferFrom hook fire before actual token transfer occurs?
What is the maximum inflation achievable without causing overflow?
Does the final validatorToken.transfer in burn() fail due to insufficient actual balance?
validation_requirements: Build ERC-777 validatorToken and test: rebalanceSwap inflates reserves → callback → burn extracts based on inflated amount
Verify that IERC20(validatorToken).transfer in burn doesn't revert due to actual balance being less than calculated amount
Calculate maximum extractable phantom reserves

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: LP tokens + enough validatorToken allowance for rebalanceSwap (tokens never actually leave attacker if callback aborts transfer)
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_execution_cost: Single transaction gas cost on L2 (~$0.50-$2.00)
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Proportional to inflation factor × attacker's LP share
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: Potentially >1000% of attacker's original LP investment

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: When pool has accumulated significant reserves from other LPs
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Pool becomes insolvent; remaining LPs cannot withdraw full value

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: All other LPs in the pool
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Partial to complete loss depending on inflation amount
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: All validatorToken reserves in excess of actual contract balance

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Overflow check at line 146 limits inflation: uint256(pool.reserveValidatorToken) + amountIn <= type(uint128).max
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Verify burn's IERC20(validatorToken).transfer will revert when actual balance < calculated amountValidatorToken from inflated reserves

threats.title: Cross-Function Reentrancy: burn() Callback Reenters executeFeeSwap() Against Un-Decremented Reserves
threats.hypothesis_description: During burn(), the first IERC20(userToken).transfer(to, amountUserToken) sends userTokens to an attacker-controlled `to` contract. Since pool.reserveValidatorToken has NOT been decremented yet, the `to` contract can approve the received userTokens to FeeAMM and reenter executeFeeSwap(userToken, validatorToken, amountUserToken). The feeSwap succeeds because reserveValidatorToken is at its pre-burn value, extracting additional validatorTokens beyond the LP's fair share. The attacker receives burn proceeds AND feeSwap proceeds in one atomic transaction.
threats.investigation_rationale: This cross-function path combines burn's transfer-before-state-update with executeFeeSwap's independent reserve check. The attacker recycles burn proceeds through feeSwap while reserves haven't been reduced, double-dipping from the pool.

proof_of_signal.pattern: IERC20(userToken).transfer(to, amountUserToken) at line 207 sends tokens to attacker before reserveValidatorToken is decremented at line 215; attacker reenters executeFeeSwap which checks stale reserveValidatorToken at line 112

preconditions: userToken supports transfer callbacks to recipient (ERC-777 tokensReceived) OR attacker uses a hook-enabled token as userToken in a permissionless pool
Attacker has LP tokens in the pool
Pool has both userToken and validatorToken reserves
`to` is attacker's contract that reenters executeFeeSwap on receiving userTokens

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: burn sends amountUserToken to `to` while reserveValidatorToken still = original. Attacker swaps received userTokens via executeFeeSwap: gets amountUserToken * 0.997 additional validatorTokens
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Recycling burn proceeds through feeSwap against stale reserves extracts validatorTokens that belong to other LPs
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Extra extraction = amountUserToken * M / SCALE validatorTokens beyond fair LP burn share

economic_questions: Can the `to` contract approve and call executeFeeSwap atomically during the transfer callback?
Does the reserveValidatorToken have enough headroom for both the burn decrement and feeSwap decrement on unwinding?
Can this be chained with same-function burn reentrancy for compound extraction?
validation_requirements: Deploy attacker contract that receives userTokens in burn → approves FeeAMM → calls executeFeeSwap
Measure total extraction vs fair burn amount
Verify no underflow on reserve updates when both burn and feeSwap decrements apply

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: Initial LP deposit to establish LP position
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Additional amountUserToken * 0.997 validatorTokens per burn cycle
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: Significant uplift on top of fair burn share (needs quantification)

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: When pool has accumulated userToken from feeSwaps (indicating active trading)
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Pool reserves depleted beyond attacker's fair share; other LPs shortchanged

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: All other LPs in the pool
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Reduced withdrawal value proportional to attacker's extra extraction

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: amountUserToken from burn = liquidity * reserveUserToken / totalSupply; additional extraction = amountUserToken * 9970 / 10000
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Final state: reserveVT decremented by both burn share AND feeSwap amountOut; verify no revert

threats.title: Unchecked transferFrom Return Value in rebalanceSwap() Allows Free Token Extraction via Optimistic State Update
threats.hypothesis_description: rebalanceSwap() performs optimistic state updates (reserveValidatorToken += amountIn, reserveUserToken -= amountOut) BEFORE calling IERC20(validatorToken).transferFrom. The transferFrom return value is not checked. If validatorToken returns false on transferFrom failure (instead of reverting), the state updates persist but no tokens arrive. The subsequent IERC20(userToken).transfer(to, amountOut) sends real userTokens to the attacker. The attacker receives userTokens without paying any validatorTokens, creating immediate insolvency for that pool.
threats.investigation_rationale: The combination of optimistic state update + unchecked return value is uniquely dangerous in rebalanceSwap. Other functions update state after transfers, limiting impact. But rebalanceSwap updates state first, then tries to pull tokens, then pushes tokens. A failed pull with an unchecked return means the push still executes.
deployment_assumptions: validatorToken returns false on transferFrom failure instead of reverting
threats.config_evidence: FeeAMM.sol uses IERC20 interface without SafeERC20 (no safeTransferFrom). No deployment script evidence of token whitelist or SafeERC20 usage. Tokens that return false include some legacy ERC-20 implementations.

proof_of_signal.pattern: State updates at lines 150-151 (pool.reserveValidatorToken += amountIn, pool.reserveUserToken -= amountOut) BEFORE IERC20(validatorToken).transferFrom at line 153; return value of transferFrom is not checked; IERC20(userToken).transfer at line 155 sends tokens regardless

preconditions: validatorToken returns false on transferFrom failure rather than reverting
Attacker calls rebalanceSwap with insufficient balance or allowance for validatorToken
Pool has userToken reserves to extract

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: State: reserveVT += amountIn (credited), reserveUT -= amountOut (debited). Transfer: transferFrom returns false (0 tokens received). Transfer: userToken.transfer sends amountOut to attacker.
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Attacker never pays validatorTokens but receives userTokens. Pool's reserveValidatorToken is inflated (credits tokens never received), reserveUserToken is deflated (debits tokens actually sent).
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Profit = amountOut of userTokens, minus 0 cost = pure profit per call

economic_questions: Which stablecoins on the target L2 return false instead of reverting?
Can the attacker repeat this indefinitely until all userToken reserves are drained?
Does the inflated reserveValidatorToken cause issues for subsequent operations?
validation_requirements: Test with a token that returns false on insufficient allowance
Verify state update persists and userTokens are sent despite failed transferFrom
Quantify maximum extraction before pool becomes unusable

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_execution_cost: Gas only (~$0.05-$0.50 per call on L2)
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Up to 100% of userToken reserves per call
preliminary_attack_economics.attack_duration: Single or multiple transactions until reserves drained

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: After pool accumulates userToken from feeSwaps
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Pool becomes insolvent; remaining operations fail

potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: 100% loss of userToken portion of their liquidity
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Entire userToken reserves in the pool

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: No SafeERC20 imported or used anywhere in contract; all transfer/transferFrom calls unchecked
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Verify on target L2 which deployed stablecoins return false vs revert

threats.title: Fee-on-Transfer Token Creates Progressive Reserve Insolvency Leading to LP Fund Loss
threats.hypothesis_description: All functions that receive tokens (executeFeeSwap, rebalanceSwap, mint) credit reserves with the nominal transfer amount (amountIn / amountValidatorToken) rather than the actual tokens received. If a fee-on-transfer token is used, the contract receives fewer tokens than recorded in reserves. Over time, reserves become increasingly inflated relative to actual balances. Eventually, burns and swaps fail when attempting to transfer more tokens than the contract holds, trapping late LPs' funds. Early LPs can exit before insolvency manifests, creating a first-mover-advantage bank run.
threats.investigation_rationale: The contract never measures actual balance changes (balanceOf before/after), instead relying entirely on nominal amounts. Any token with a transfer fee creates a growing deficit between recorded reserves and actual balances.
deployment_assumptions: Pool is created with a fee-on-transfer token as either userToken or validatorToken
threats.config_evidence: FeeAMM.sol lines 114-117 (executeFeeSwap), 150-155 (rebalanceSwap), 183-185 (mint) all update reserves by nominal amounts without balance verification. No deployment script indicates token restrictions.

proof_of_signal.pattern: pool.reserveUserToken += uint128(amountIn) credits full nominal amount; no balanceOf check to verify actual received amount; same pattern in mint at line 185 and rebalanceSwap at line 150

preconditions: At least one token in the pool charges a transfer fee
Multiple transactions accumulate deficit between reserves and actual balances
Pool has enough activity for the deficit to become material

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: If token has 1% fee: each mint of 10000 tokens credits 10000 to reserves but only 9900 arrive. After 10 mints: reserves show 100000, actual balance = 99000. Deficit = 1000 tokens.
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Early LP exits (burn) can withdraw full pro-rata share while actual balance still covers it. Later LPs find withdrawals revert due to insufficient actual balance.
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: An informed attacker deposits and immediately withdraws, extracting more than their proportional share of actual tokens at the expense of existing LPs

economic_questions: Are any TIP-20 stablecoins fee-on-transfer?
How quickly does the deficit grow to cause reverts?
Can an attacker accelerate the deficit by cycling deposits and withdrawals?
validation_requirements: Test with a fee-on-transfer token and measure reserve vs balance divergence
Calculate number of transactions before first burn failure
Verify early withdrawers can extract at expense of late withdrawers

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: Large LP deposit to be the early withdrawer
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Proportional to fee percentage × total pool volume
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: Variable; depends on fee percentage and pool activity
preliminary_attack_economics.attack_duration: Multi-transaction over time, or accelerated via rapid cycling

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: As soon as deficit is large enough for profitable exit
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Pool becomes permanently insolvent; last LP unable to withdraw

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: Late LPs who cannot fully withdraw
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Proportional to cumulative transfer fee deficit
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Cumulative fee-on-transfer deficit across all transactions

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: No balance-before/after pattern anywhere in contract; pure nominal accounting
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Test deficit accumulation rate with 0.1%, 1%, and 2% transfer fee tokens

threats.title: Non-Returning ERC20 Tokens (USDT-like) Cause Complete Pool Operation Failure
threats.hypothesis_description: The contract calls IERC20(token).transfer() and IERC20(token).transferFrom() which expect a bool return value per the IERC20 interface. In Solidity 0.8, the ABI decoder strictly requires return data matching the expected type. Tokens that don't return a value (like USDT on Ethereum mainnet) will cause ABI decoding to revert because the returndata is empty. If such a token is used in a pool, ALL operations (mint, burn, swaps) will permanently revert, trapping any funds already deposited.
threats.investigation_rationale: The contract imports only IERC20 (not SafeERC20 which handles non-returning tokens). Solidity 0.8's strict decoding treats missing return data as a revert. If any pool uses a non-returning token, deposits via mint would revert (preventing new deposits), but if any token balances were sent directly, they'd be permanently locked.
deployment_assumptions: Pool token does not return bool from transfer/transferFrom (e.g., USDT on certain chains)
threats.config_evidence: FeeAMM.sol imports only @openzeppelin/contracts/token/ERC20/IERC20.sol (line 4). No SafeERC20 imported. foundry.toml shows no chain-specific configuration. On L2s (Base/Optimism/Arbitrum), bridged USDT typically returns bool, reducing this risk on L2 deployments.

proof_of_signal.pattern: import IERC20 (not SafeERC20); all transfer/transferFrom calls expect bool return; Solidity 0.8 strict ABI decoding reverts on empty returndata

preconditions: Pool uses a token that doesn't return bool from transfer/transferFrom
Contract is deployed on a chain where such tokens exist (more likely L1 than L2)

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: IERC20(token).transfer(...) compiles to: CALL + ABI decode bool from returndata. If returndata.length == 0, decoder reverts.
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Not an attack per se; a compatibility failure that locks funds if any reach the contract
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: No direct profit; denial of service and potential fund loss for users who interact with incompatible pools

economic_questions: Which stablecoins on the target L2 don't return bool?
Can funds be sent to the contract via direct transfer (not through protocol functions) and get stuck?
Is there any recovery mechanism?
validation_requirements: Test with USDT-like token that returns no data
Verify all protocol functions revert
Check if direct ERC20 transfers to the contract can be recovered

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: No direct profit; DoS and fund locking

market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Pools with affected tokens completely non-functional

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: Users attempting to create or interact with pools using non-returning tokens
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Wasted gas; potential fund loss if tokens sent directly to contract
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Any tokens directly transferred to contract address

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Verify which tokens on target chain return bool vs no data
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Test mint, executeFeeSwap, rebalanceSwap, burn with non-returning token

threats.title: Zero-Value Token Transfer in burn() Permanently Locks LP Funds When Token Rejects Zero Transfers
threats.hypothesis_description: When a pool has zero reserveUserToken (e.g., all userToken was removed via rebalanceSwap, or before any feeSwaps occurred), burn() calculates amountUserToken = liquidity * 0 / totalSupply = 0. It then calls IERC20(userToken).transfer(to, 0). Some ERC-20 implementations revert on zero-value transfers. If userToken is such a token, all burn() calls revert, permanently locking LP funds in the contract. The pool has validatorToken reserves that LPs cannot access.
threats.investigation_rationale: The burn function does not check if calculated amounts are zero before calling transfer. A zero reserveUserToken is a normal state (pool just created, or fully rebalanced). If the userToken reverts on zero transfer, LPs are permanently locked out.
deployment_assumptions: userToken reverts on zero-value transfer calls
threats.config_evidence: FeeAMM.sol burn() at lines 207-208 calls transfer for both tokens without checking for zero amounts. The _calculateBurnAmounts function (lines 227-234) can return 0 for either amount when the corresponding reserve is 0.

proof_of_signal.pattern: _calculateBurnAmounts returns amountUserToken = (liquidity * pool.reserveUserToken) / _totalSupply; when reserveUserToken = 0, result = 0; IERC20(userToken).transfer(to, 0) may revert on some tokens

preconditions: Pool has reserveUserToken == 0 (normal state before any feeSwaps or after full rebalancing)
userToken implementation reverts on transfer(to, 0)
LPs hold LP tokens they wish to burn

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: amountUserToken = liquidity * 0 / totalSupply = 0; IERC20(userToken).transfer(to, 0) → potential revert
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Not attacker-initiated; natural pool state can cause this. However, an attacker could intentionally fully rebalance a pool to zero userToken, then all LPs are locked.
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Griefing: attacker performs rebalanceSwap to drain all userToken from the pool, locking all LPs. Attacker profits indirectly if they short the protocol's reputation/token.

economic_questions: Which TIP-20 stablecoins revert on zero-value transfer?
How easily can reserveUserToken be driven to zero via rebalanceSwap?
Is there any recovery path for locked LP funds?
validation_requirements: Test burn with reserveUserToken = 0 using a zero-transfer-reverting token
Verify LP funds are permanently locked
Check if any function can restore userToken reserves to enable withdrawal

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: Cost of rebalanceSwap to drain userToken (variable)
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: No direct profit; griefing attack locks LP funds

market_conditions_analysis.liquidity_requirements: Attacker needs validatorToken to execute rebalanceSwap
market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: When pool has accumulated significant LP deposits but userToken can be fully drained
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: All LP funds in the affected pool permanently locked

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Identify which tokens revert on zero transfer; test rebalanceSwap to fully drain userToken
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Verify zero-value transfer behavior of target stablecoins
